paper_title: $V_{0.5}$: Generalist Value Model as a Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), constructing a robust advantage baseline is critical for policy gradients, effectively guiding the policy model to reinforce desired behaviors. Recent research has introduced Generalist Value Models (such as $V_0$), which achieve pre-trained value estimation by explicitly encoding model capabilities in-context, eliminating the need to synchronously update the value model alongside the policy model. In this paper, we propose $V_{0.5}$, which adaptively fuses the baseline predicted by such value model (acting as a prior) with the empirical mean derived from sparse rollouts. This constructs a robust baseline that balances computational efficiency with extremely low variance. Specifically, we introduce a real-time statistical testing and dynamic budget allocation. This balances the high variance caused by sparse sampling against the systematic bias (or hallucinations) inherent in the value model's prior. By constructing a hypothesis test to evaluate the prior's reliability in real-time, the system dynamically allocates additional rollout budget on demand. This mechanism minimizes the baseline estimator's Mean Squared Error (MSE), guaranteeing stable policy gradients, even under extreme sparsity with a group size of 4. Extensive evaluations across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that $V_{0.5}$ significantly outperforms GRPO and DAPO, achieving faster convergence and over some 10% performance improvement.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Yi-Kai Zhang1,2,3, Yueqing Sun3, Hongyan Hao3, Qi Gu3,#, Xunliang Cai3, De-Chuan Zhan1,2, Han-Jia Ye1,2,# 1School of Artificial Intelligence, Nanjing University 2National Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology, Nanjing University 3Meituan, China Project Page: https://now-join-us.github.io/V0_

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), constructing robust advantage baseline is critical for policy gradients, effectively guiding the policy model to reinforce desired behaviors. Recent research has introduced Generalist Value Models (such as V0), which achieve pre-trained value estimation by explicitly encoding model capabilities in-context, eliminating the need to synchronously update the value model alongside the policy model. In this paper, we propose V0.5, which adaptively fuses the baseline predicted by such value model (acting as prior) with the empirical mean derived from sparse rollouts. This constructs robust baseline that balances computational efficiency with extremely low variance. Specifically, we introduce real-time statistical testing and dynamic budget allocation. This balances the high variance caused by sparse sampling against the systematic bias (or hallucinations) inherent in the value models prior. By constructing hypothesis test to evaluate the priors reliability in real-time, the system dynamically allocates additional rollout budget on demand. This mechanism minimizes the baseline estimators Mean Squared Error (MSE), guaranteeing stable policy gradients, even under extreme sparsity with group size of 4. Extensive evaluations across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that V0.5 significantly outperforms GRPO and DAPO, achieving faster convergence and over some 10% performance improvement. 6 2 0 2 1 1 ] . [ 1 8 4 8 0 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r Figure 1. Performance of 0.5 across six diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, demonstrates superiority over GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) and DAPO (Yu et al., 2025), achieving faster convergence and some over 10% performance improvement. # Correspondence to guqi03@meituan.com and yehj@lamda.nju.edu.cn. 1 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts 1. Introduction In the post-training phase of Large Language Models (LLMs), Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as standard paradigm for enhancing complex reasoning capabilities (Team et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2025; Comanici et al., 2025). For policy gradient methods within this paradigm, constructing robust advantage baseline is critical for stable training (Williams, 1992; Greensmith et al., 2004; Schulman et al., 2018). Currently, baseline estimation relies on two primary approaches: (1) Monte Carlo Sampling (e.g., GRPO (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025)), which computes the empirical reward mean from online rollouts, and (2) Parameterized Value Models (e.g., PPO (Schulman et al., 2017)), which predict the expected return via separate model. Both face significant limitations. While empirical sampling guarantees an unbiased estimate, the high computational cost of long-horizon tasks forces the use of sparse rollouts. This sparsity exposes the empirical mean to severe statistical variance, which can compromise training stability. Conversely, while parameterized value models reduce this variance, they require expensive synchronous training and are prone to introducing systematic bias due to poor out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Recent research highlights the potential of Generalist Value Models, such as V0 (Zhang et al., 2026). By explicitly encoding model capabilities in-context, V0 is capable of estimating the expected performance of any model on unseen prompts without requiring parameter updates. Rather than synchronously training coupled critic, it generates predictions via completely separate, frozen model. Because this value estimation is generated independently of, and prior to, the actual online rollouts, it functions as statistical prior for the advantage baseline. This prior may provide immediate guidance under sparse rollout conditions. However, like any generalist model, V0 is susceptible to hallucinations or biased predictions when evaluating novel or overly complex out-of-distribution (OOD) prompts. Therefore, critical challenge emerges: how can we safely integrate this static prior into the empirical baseline estimation of sparse online rollouts, ensuring that the policy model benefits from the priors variance-reduction properties without being corrupted by its occasional hallucinations? In this paper, we propose 0.5, an adaptive baseline estimation and budget allocation framework designed to systematically resolve this dilemma. V0.5 operates through two tightly coupled mechanisms: (1) it safely fuses the value models prior with empirical sparse rollouts to construct more robust advantage estimation; (2), it adaptively scales the rollout budget based on the real-time statistical conflict between the prior predictions and the online observations. The framework is designed to fully exploit the generalist prior to suppress the high variance of sparse sampling when the predictions are reliable, while strictly bounding the impact of hallucinations by isolating the prior and dynamically allocating additional budget only when statistically necessary to correct the baseline. Specifically, V0.5 achieves this through the following components: Empirical Shrinkage Fusion: For fixed number of sparse rollouts, V0.5 constructs baseline using shrinkage estimator that fuses the empirical mean with the prior prediction. We demonstrate that the mean squared error (MSE) of this combined baseline orthogonally decomposes into observation variance and prior bias. To protect against severe prior bias, V0.5 employs positive-part truncation functionally equivalent to statistical hypothesis test. It dynamically assesses the value models reliability: if consistent with the rollouts, the prior is heavily leveraged to suppress variance; if severe conflict is detected (indicating hallucination), the system swiftly isolates the prior and reverts to the empirical mean, guaranteeing safe, bounded error (James et al., 1961). Sequential OSLA Allocation: Relying solely on rigid sparse rollouts can lead to false rejections of an accurate prior due to the sheer randomness of limited sampling. To address this, V0.5 extends baseline estimation into dynamic budget allocation problem. Grounded in One-Step-Look-Ahead (OSLA) sequential analysis, the framework quantifies baseline uncertainty in real-time to derive continuous target boundary. This allows the system to automatically decide whether to halt sampling early to save budget, or to enforce additional rollouts to resolve conflicts and correct prior hallucinations. By balancing statistical precision with marginal costs, it achieves optimal on-demand scheduling (Wald, 2004). Our main contributions are summarized as follows: We propose V0.5 to safely integrate generalist value priors into sparse RL rollouts. By employing an empirical shrinkage estimator coupled with the Sequential OSLA Allocation, the framework neutralizes the high variance of limited rollouts while actively safeguarding against hallucinations of the value model. We provide mathematical foundations for both mechanisms. We prove that the baseline mean squared error (MSE) orthogonally decomposes to linearly suppress the overall policy gradient variance, and we establish the asymptotic optimality of our dynamic stopping rule. Extensive evaluations validate the practical impact of our approach. By enabling robust, on-demand budget scheduling, V0.5 significantly outperforms standard GRPO, achieving over 10% performance improvement across six diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks. 2 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts 2. Preliminaries Before detailing the V0.5 framework, this section formalizes the policy gradient objective within Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). We contrast the two dominant baseline estimation paradigms, coupled value models and empirical group sampling, to highlight their respective structural limitations. Subsequently, we formalize the statistical bias-variance dilemma that requires the fusion of sparse online rollouts with generalist prior. 2.1. Policy Gradients and the Baseline During LLM post-training, policy model πθ generates response for prompt Dprompt, receiving scalar reward from verifier or reward function. For simplicity, we assume normalized binary rewards, {1, 1}. To reduce policy gradient variance, baseline µ is subtracted from the reward to compute the advantage (A = µ), ensuring the model reinforces behaviors that exceed its average capability. Theoretically, the optimal baseline is the true expected return for x: µtrue = Eoπθ [r x] (1) Since computing µtrue over the vast generation space of LLMs is computationally infeasible, the baseline should be approximated. Two primary paradigms currently exist, but both face distinct limitations in complex reasoning tasks: Parameterized Value Models (e.g., PPO (Schulman et al., 2017)): Actor-Critic architectures maintain parameterized value function Vϕ to estimate expected returns. While effective at variance reduction, this creates coupling dilemma: the value model must be incrementally and synchronously trained to track the non-stationary target of the evolving policy πθ, incurring substantial computational and memory overhead. Empirical Group Sampling (e.g., GRPO (Shao et al., 2024)): Value-free methods eliminate the independent value model. Instead, for given prompt x, the policy generates group of independent candidate responses, {o1, o2, . . . , oG}. The baseline is approximated via their empirical mean: vG = 1 (cid:88) k=1 rk (2) Although vG is an unbiased estimate of µtrue, its observation variance is inversely proportional to the group size (σ2 noise 1/G). Preventing high variance or reward collapse (uniform -1 or 1 rewards) requires extensive Monte Carlo sampling. For long-horizon tasks, relying on large is prohibitive. Consequently, under constrained sparse rollout regime, the amplified variance causes the baseline estimate to fluctuate, yielding gradients that can destabilize training. 2.2. Generalist Value Models: Breaking the Coupling Dilemma To avoid the synchronous training overhead of traditional critics, recent research has introduced Generalist Value Models, such as V0. These models reframe value estimation by shifting from implicit parameter fitting to explicit In-Context Learning (ICL). Rather than embedding policy information into latent weights, generalist model treats the capability of an arbitrary policy π explicitly via context set Cπ = {(xi, ri)}N i=1 of historical query-performance pairs. Given target prompt x, the generalist model infers the expected return dynamically: This allows the value model to read the current capabilities of any policy and provide baseline estimate before any online rollouts are generated, effectively decoupling value estimation from policy evolution without requiring gradient updates. = V0(x, Cπ) (3) 2.3. The Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Sparse Rollouts While generalist prior provides immediate guidance and resolves the coupling dilemma, utilizing it directly as the baseline introduces distinct limitation. Operating as static prior, possesses an observation variance of zero; however, constrained by the generalist models inherent generalization limits, it is susceptible to prediction errors on out-of-distribution (OOD) prompts. This introduces systematic prior bias, quantified as: 2 = (V µtrue) (4) If left unmitigated, these prior estimation errors systematically corrupt advantage calculation. Consequently, baseline estimation under sparse rollouts is trapped in strict statistical tradeoff: 3 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Empirical Mean vG = 1 Prior Prediction = V0(x, Cπ): Stable (Variance = 0), but susceptible to systematic errors. k=1 rk: Unbiased (Bias = 0), but subject to high variance under sparse rollouts. (cid:80)G Given that the ground truth µtrue is unknown, relying exclusively on either the empirical mean or the potentially biased prior is suboptimal. This establishes the fundamental necessity for the V0.5 framework. It must intelligently fuse these two estimators, leveraging the prior to suppress rollout variance while dynamically allocating additional rollout budget when statistical conflicts indicate prior inaccuracy. 2.4. Unified Advantage Formulations To clearly contrast the existing paradigms and set the stage for our proposed solution, we present the unified expressions for the advantage Ai = A(x, oi) computed for specific rollout oi 1. Coupled Value Model (PPO): Ai = ri Vϕ(x). Empirical Sampling (GRPO): Ai = ri Our Proposed V0.5: (cid:16) 1 (cid:80)G k=1 rk (cid:17) . Ai = ri (w vk + (1 w) V0(x, Cπ)) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) Fused Shrinkage Baseline µ (cid:124) (5) The V0.5 estimates the baseline through convex combination of the empirical mean and the generalist prior. In section 3, we will rigorously detail the mathematical motivation for this specific formulation, prove how it minimizes the Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the baseline estimator, and explain how the adaptive weight is dynamically computed in real-time. 3. Method The V0.5 aims to resolve the limitations of empirical mean and prior prediction as baselines in sparse rollout scenarios through statistical inference and sequential decision-making mechanisms. This section first outlines the core execution logic of V0.5, and then delves into the mathematical motivation and theoretical derivations supporting this logic. 3.1. Core Execution Logic of V0.5 The logic of V0.5 transforms baseline estimation into dynamic process of statistical testing and budget allocation. For given input prompt, the system operates according to the following workflow: 1. Before generating rollouts, the generalist value model V0 outputs prior prediction as the expected return. 2. The policy model generates small initial set of kinit rollouts, and calculates the empirical mean vk. 3. Deviation testing & fusion: The system calculates the guaranteed variance estimate ˆσ2 the empirical bias ˆ2 fuses the empirical mean with the prior prediction via Equation 9 to obtain the current baseline estimate. noise according to Equation 6 and according to Equation 7. Next, it computes the adaptive fusion weight ˆwk using Equation 8, and 4. Dynamic budget allocation: Based on the current empirical bias ˆ k, the system evaluates the baseline in real-time and determines the subsequent action: Stop: If the fused baseline satisfies the criteria of the optimal stopping rule in Equation 12, the system halts the rollout process and outputs the advantage. Rollout More: If the test reveals significant bias, the system forces the policy model to generate additional rollouts and loops back to Step 3 to recalculate the fused baseline. As this process iterates, the system may progressively shift its reliance toward the empirical mean. Next, we detail the mathematical motivation and derivations that drive this logic. 3.2. Motivation: Propagation Limits of Baseline MSE on Gradient Variance Before detailing our fusion algorithm, we must clarify the theoretical optimization objective: how does the baselines MSE amplify policy gradient variance? To this end, we establish the analytical relationship between the theoretical MSE of baseline estimator b, denoted as MSE(b) = E[(b µtrue)2], and the overall variance of the policy gradient. 1While GRPO and V0.5 further normalize by dividing by the standard deviation of the groups rewards, we omit this for clarity. 4 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Figure 2. Demonstration of PPO, GRPO, and our proposed V0.5 framework. While PPO requires synchronously trained value model and GRPO relies on the empirical group mean, V0.5 computes an adaptive baseline by fusing prior from frozen generalist value model (V0) with sparse empirical rollouts via dynamic weight (detailed in Theorem 3.3, Equation 6, and Equation 7). Theorem 3.1. For single-step policy gradient estimator ˆg(θ) using baseline b, the trace of its covariance matrix is strictly bounded by: Tr(Var(ˆg(θ))) Varoracle + Φscore MSE(b) + Bias(b) where: Varoracle: The theoretical minimum variance achieved by perfect baseline (b = µtrue). Φscore: The expected squared norm of the policys score function (Eπ[θ log πθ(yx)2]). L: constant scaling the cross-perturbation penalty introduced by the baselines bias. Analysis and Insights: For LLMs with billions of parameters, the gradient sensitivity scale Φscore is inherently massive. Theorem 3.1 directly reveals that under sparse rollouts, any estimation error (MSE(b)) is severely amplified by Φscore, causing the gradient variance to explode. This dictates the core design philosophy of V0.5: rather than chasing an unbiased but highly noisy empirical baseline, we must safely integrate prior to intentionally tolerate minor, mathematically bounded bias Bias(b) in exchange for massive reduction in MSE(b). 3.3. Empirical Shrinkage Fusion To minimize the theoretical MSE under fixed number of sparse samples, we construct Shrinkage Estimator that fuses the empirical mean vk and the prior prediction via convex combination: µ = wvk + (1 w)V . By substituting this into the MSE, we establish the following theorem. 5 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Theorem 3.2. Since the prior is deterministic before rollout and vk is an unbiased estimator, the MSE of this shrinkage estimator can be decomposed into weighted sum of empirical variance and prior bias: MSE(w) = E[(µ µtrue)2] = w2σ2 noise + (1 w)22 With the error decomposed, we can pinpoint the exact weight that minimizes the overall MSE, as shown in Theorem 3.3. We must approximate them using real-time observations to construct an empirical estimator. We denote these with hat (e.g., ˆσ noise, ˆ2 k, ˆwk). 1. Thus, the variance of the empirical mean vk over independent rollouts is bounded by 1/k, which we use as our guaranteed estimate: 2. For the detailed statistical derivation establishing this equivalence, please refer to subsection A.4. BIAS BOUNDS OF THE ESTIMATOR Unlike µ which uses the optimal weight w, our empirical weight ˆwk is dependent on the random variable vk. This statistical correlation renders the fused estimator ˆµ biased. However, Theorem 3.4 guarantees that this induced bias is strictly confined within safe analytical limits. Theorem 3.4. The empirical baseline estimator ˆµ possesses the following safety properties: 1. Bias(ˆµ) 1 . 2. If = 0, the induced bias decays at O( 1 ) as the increases. 6 (8) (9) ˆσ with ˆσ = (cid:112)1 ˆµ2, V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Recalling Theorem 3.1, we observe that by allowing bounded bias of at most O(1/ k), the V0.5 estimator effectively offsets the catastrophic O(1/k) amplification of the MSE, ensuring stable training gradients even under extreme sparsity. 3.4. Sequential OSLA Allocation and Optimal Stopping While static fusion optimally balances bias and variance under fixed compute, relying purely on rigid sample size under extreme sparsity can result in high observational noise, potentially leading to false rejections of an accurate prior. To resolve this, V0.5 integrates dynamic sequential One-Step-Look-Ahead (OSLA) allocation mechanism. We define the total risk after sampling steps as combination of the empirical estimation error and the compute cost: R(k) = (cid:91)MSE(k) + where (cid:91)MSE(k) = ˆ2 ˆ2 + 1 (11) Here, denotes the compute cost factor per rollout. Crucially, (cid:91)MSE(k) represents the empirical MSE, calculated by substituting the unknown 2 with our real-time empirical estimate ˆ2 k. The decision to allocate additional budget hinges on whether the expected reduction in this empirical error exceeds the marginal cost. Lemma 3.5. By substituting the group size in Equation 10 with 4. Experiments 4.1. Experimental Setup and Implementation Details 4.1.1. INTRODUCTION TO THE GENERALIST VALUE MODEL (V0) The Generalist Value Model (V0) avoids the synchronous gradient updates characteristic of traditional Actor-Critic architectures. Instead of implicitly fitting the policys evolving capabilities, V0 explicitly represents these capabilities using context of historical query-performance pairs, denoted as Cπ = {(xi, ri)}. Consequently, the traditional value estimation π(x) is reformulated as (Cπ, x). This paradigm allows the value model to be fully pre-trained offline, providing zero-gradient advantage baselines during the RL phase. The network architecture of V0 consists of: Semantic-Perception Backbone: Uses an LLM embedding model to map instructions into semantic vectors. Residual Query Adapter: Employs learnable queries and residual mechanism to project the entangled semantic features into structured, compressed latent space. Probabilistic In-Context Head: Uses TabPFN to perform single-pass Bayesian inference on the historical pairs Cπ, yielding the predicted success probability of the target query. V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Figure 3. Evolution of policy gradient norm. V0.5 maintains lower and more stable gradient norm than GRPO. By trading strictly bounded bias for reduced baseline MSE, it effectively neutralizes the variance amplification inherent in sparse rollouts. Figure 4. Evolution of policy entropy. While GRPOs high-variance gradients cause rapid entropy decay, V0.5 leverages low-noise baseline estimation to sustain higher entropy, ensuring robust exploration in reasoning tasks. 4.1.2. ENHANCED TRAINING OF V0 To support complex mathematical reasoning tasks, we trained an enhanced V0 model. Data Construction. The value model requires diverse performance data to capture wide range of capabilities. We sampled GRPO training trajectories from LLMs across various architectural scales. Each trajectory contained over 200 checkpoints, with about 20k rollouts per checkpoint. Building upon the models used for the original V0 (Qwen3-4B-Instruct2507, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B), we expanded the pool to include the full Qwen3 series (0.6B to 30B parameters), with Base, Instruct, and Thinking variants. This data synthesis produced approximately 424k high-quality training pairs reflecting rich evolutionary histories and performance disparities. Model Architecture and Hyperparameters. We retained the base V0 architecture. The backbone utilizes the frozen Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B (dembed = 1024). The adapter is configured with 168 static queries, projection dimension of 6, and 3 Multi-Head Attention (MHA) layers. The inference head employs TabPFN-v2.5. During training, we randomly sample 256 query-performance pairs to form the capability context Cπ. The enhanced model was pre-trained on 128 GPUs for approximately 40 hours. All other hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, loss ratios) remain identical to the original V0. 4.1.3. ALGORITHMIC WORKFLOW AND IMPLEMENTATION OF V0.5 The V0.5 framework achieves low variance and adaptive compute scheduling in sparse rollout scenarios by tightly integrating Empirical Shrinkage Fusion with Sequential OSLA Allocation. Step 1: Construct Context and Acquire Prior . The system maintains global Support Buffer (capacity 512) storing recent sample performances (Cπ). It randomly samples 256 pairs (Support Batch Size) and queries the value model API in batches (Query Batch Size) to retrieve the target querys predicted success probability [0, 1]. Step 2: Cold Start Allocation. An initial baseline rollout is executed for all batch prompts. To ensure robust hypothesis V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Figure 5. Performance of V0.5 under extreme sparsity (1, 2, 4, and 8 rollouts) vs. standard GRPO (16 rollouts). To ensure fair comparison, prompt batch sizes are adjusted to maintain constant per-step computational overhead. testing in the discrete binary reward space {1, 1}, we set the initial group size to kinit = 4. As detailed in subsection A.8, this size guarantees the tests tolerance radius fully absorbs the observation gap caused by discrete sampling. Step 3: Initial Bias Evaluation and Hypothesis Testing. The system uses the empirical mean v4 to calculate the squared observation bias (v4 )2 against the theoretical noise upper bound 1/4. If (v4 )2 1/4, the prior is deemed highly reliable ( ˆ2 = 0), the deviation is attributed to observation noise, and no extra compute is required. Conversely, if ˆ2 > 0, it indicates potential value model hallucination, triggering additional budget allocation. Step 4: Sequential OSLA Allocation and Alignment. For samples exhibiting bias, the system calculates compute 16. The dynamic target compute is boundary. Given marginal step cost = 0.0039, the maximum budget is 1/ defined as ktarget = 1/ k. If the current observation count < ktarget, 2 additional rollouts are allocated. 1/ ˆ2 To maximize distributed hardware utilization, dynamic generation halts globally if fewer than 25% of the batch samples require additional compute. To prevent resource fragmentation during Tensor Parallelism, the allocated budget for single dispatch is automatically padded to multiple of 32. 9 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Step 5: Final Empirical Shrinkage Fusion and Advantage Calculation. Once dynamic allocation concludes, the system collects the final observations (4 16) per prompt. It updates the empirical mean vk, the guaranteed variance estimate ˆσ2 k. The system then calculates the adaptive shrinkage weight ˆwk to derive the low-variance fused baseline ˆµ and deduces the intrinsic standard deviation ˆσ = (cid:112)1 (ˆµ)2. The final standardized advantage, Ai = (ri ˆµ)/ˆσ, is passed to the surrogate objective function to update the policy. noise, and the empirical bias ˆ2 4.1.4. TRAINING HYPERPARAMETERS, BASELINE CONFIGURATIONS, AND EVALUATION METRICS Training Hyperparameters. We conducted all RL training on 4 nodes (32 GPUs total) using the sglang engine. The base policy is Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, fine-tuned on the DAPO-Math-17k dataset (Yu et al., 2025). The Actor uses the AdamW optimizer with learning rate of 1 106, 10 warmup steps, no KL divergence penalty, no learning rate decay, and global gradient clipping of 1.0. For both training and evaluation, the maximum response length is 4096. To ensure fair comparisons, we maintained constant total computational overhead per step across all settings. Specifically, the product of the global prompt batch size and the group size per prompt is held constant. Baseline Configurations. All baselines operate under identical hardware and software frameworks: GRPO: Uses global prompt batch size of 512, fixed group size = 16, and KL penalty coefficient of 0.001. DAPO: Uses the same batch size of 512, group size = 16, and introduces DAPO-specific advantage filtering and asymmetric clipping. The rollout generation batch size is 512 3. Evaluation Metrics We evaluated performance across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks: AIME 2024 (of America, 2024), AIME 2025 (of America, 2025), Olympiad Bench (He et al., 2024), MATH500 (Hendrycks et al., 2021), Minerva Math (Lewkowycz et al., 2022), and AMC 2023 (Math-AI, 2025). We report the average dataset accuracy using fixed sampling size of 16 (mean@16). Both training and evaluation rely strictly on rule-based rewards for correctness verification, omitting formatting rewards. Evaluation inference uses temperature of 1.0 and Top-p of 0.7. Figure 1: Shows the performance of V0.5 with the complete OSLA dynamic budget allocation enabled (kinit = 4). Figure 5: Compares V0.5 without OSLA (using fixed group sizes of 1, 2, 4, and 8) against standard GRPO (G = 16). 4.2. Main Results and Theoretical Validation This section analyzes the comprehensive performance of the V0.5 framework under the OSLA dynamic budget allocation setting (kinit = 4). Furthermore, we dissect its core advantages in gradient stability and policy exploration through the lens of our theoretical derivations. Overall Performance and Convergence Speed. As illustrated in Figure 1, we evaluated V0.5 with full OSLA dynamic budget allocation across six diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The results demonstrate the significant superiority of V0.5: compared to GRPO and DAPO, V0.5 not only achieves faster convergence but also attains over 10% improvement in final accuracy. By introducing generalist value model as prior, the policy model receives high-quality advantage estimation guidance early in the sparse rollouts, substantially enhancing sample efficiency and final performance. Mathematical Mechanism of Gradient Norm and Variance Reduction. Figure 3 illustrates the evolution of the policy gradient norm during training. It is clearly observable that, compared to GRPO, the V0.5 framework maintains lower and more stable gradient norm. This phenomenon is explained by the Theorem 3.1 (proof in subsection A.2). In LLM training, the expected squared norm of the score function, Φscore, is inherently massive. GRPO relies solely on extremely sparse empirical sampling, leading to an exceptionally high MSE(b). This high error is severely amplified by the massive Φscore, inevitably causing gradient variance explosion and norm oscillation. V0.5 employs Empirical Shrinkage Fusion to integrate the prior , strategically trading strictly bounded minor bias for reduction in variance. It intentionally tolerates mathematically constrained bias (Bias(ˆµ) 1 ) to exchange for drastic reduction in MSE(b). This design neutralizes the catastrophic amplification effect caused by Φscore, guaranteeing the numerical stability of gradients under extreme sparsity and high noise. Policy Entropy Maintenance and Exploration Capability The effective reduction of gradient variance not only stabilizes the training process but also directly enhances the policys exploration behavior. Figure 4 shows the evolution of policy 10 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts entropy over training steps. Under sparse rollout scenarios, GRPOs policy entropy decays rapidly. This occurs because highvariance, erroneous gradient signals may force the model into local optima. Conversely, benefiting from its low-variance, low-noise gradient estimation, the V0.5 framework sustains higher entropy level throughout the training cycle. This ensures that the model can robustly conduct exploration within the complex mathematical reasoning space. Performance under Extreme Sparsity and Limitations of Group Size. In Figure 5, we tested sparse group sizes (k {1, 2, 4, 8}) without OSLA dynamic allocation. Notably, simply fusing the prior with sparse rollouts (k {4, 8}) is sufficient to outperform standard GRPO (G = 16), strongly validating the core variance reduction of our shrinkage estimator. However, training fails to converge at extreme sparsity (k {1, 2}). This aligns with our base group size derivations (subsection A.8). In the binary reward space {1, 1}, our hypothesis test uses theoretical maximum noise bound (1/ k) to absorb normal observation fluctuations. For {1, 2}, the large discrete quantization gaps between possible empirical means exceed this tolerance radius. Consequently, normal sampling variance frequently triggers false rejections of the prior . Discarding this stabilizing prior in favor of highly noisy empirical mean induces severe gradient variance. 5. Related Work In RL for LLMs, the estimation quality of the advantage baseline directly determines the variance and stability of policy gradient updates. To eliminate the substantial computational overhead introduced by auxiliary value models in traditional PPO (Schulman et al., 2017; Yue et al., 2025), GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) proposes directly utilizing the average reward of intra-group samples as an empirical baseline. Concurrently, ReMax (Li et al., 2024) replaces the value network with rewards obtained from the models own greedy decoding, while OPO (Hao et al., 2025) derives an optimal baseline approximation using response-length-weighted average rewards, grounded in the assumption of gradient orthogonality. However, standard empirical means can be sensitive to outlying rewards, often failing in sparse-rollout scenarios. To address this, MC-GRPO (Kim, 2026) introduces the median baseline and the Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) to resist outliers that trigger advantage sign flipping. QAE (Wu et al., 2026) designs K-quantile dual-state gated baseline to filter noise and prevent entropy explosion, whereas BNPO (Xiao et al., 2025) models expected rewards as Beta distribution, dynamically computing optimal normalization parameters via moment estimation. Further research reveals that relying solely on intra-group sampling introduces statistical biases. For instance, HA-DW (Yang et al., 2026) demonstrates that group means underestimate the advantage of difficult prompts, thus introducing Kalman filter-based history-aware anchors. Moreover, targeting ambiguous credit assignment in long-horizon or structured tasks, Turn-PPO (Li et al., 2026) advocates aligning actions and states to physical turns to provide turn-level baselines. GiGPO (Feng et al., 2025) designs bi-level relative advantage baseline combining global normalization with micro-anchor grouping, and Tree-OPO (Huang et al., 2025) formulates advantage computation as constrained quadratic programming problem that respects the topological logic of Monte Carlo trees. Despite significant advancements in lightweight design, robustness, and bias control, these methods may remain constrained by high variance and estimation bias in extreme sparse-sampling environments when relying exclusively on empirical data. Our V0.5 elegantly resolves this dilemma by introducing the Generalist Value Model (V0) as an explicit prior. It innovatively incorporates real-time statistical testing and dynamic One-Step-Look-Ahead (OSLA) sequential budget allocation to adaptively fuse sparse empirical means with prior predictions. 6. Conclusion In this paper, we introduced the V0.5 framework, representing the next-generation evolution of generalist value models. By seamlessly uniting adaptive baseline estimation with dynamic budget allocation, this framework robustly optimizes advantage estimation with the generalist value model as statistical prior. We demonstrate that the proposed Empirical Shrinkage Fusion effectively minimizes the baselines Mean Squared Error (MSE). Safeguarded by real-time hypothesis testing, V0.5 bounds the negative effects of prior hallucinations, ensuring stable policy gradient convergence even under extreme sparsity with group size of merely 4. Furthermore, our Sequential OSLA Allocation mechanism reframes baseline estimation as continuous dynamic scheduling problem, enabling the automatic, on-demand adjustment of rollout budgets. Extensive evaluations across six diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks establish that V0.5 outperforms both GRPO and DAPO, achieving faster convergence and some over 10% performance improvement. Looking ahead, we aim to construct and pre-train Process-level Generalist Value Model. By providing finer-grained guidance signals for trajectories, we expect substantial breakthroughs in enhancing the exploration efficiency of increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks. 11 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts

sections.title: Acknowledgments
sections.content: The authors would like to thank Ying Zeng from Peking University for the valuable discussions on the theoretical analysis.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: A. Theoretical Analysis and Proofs A.1. Extended Statistical Motivation (subsection 3.1) Objective: In subsection 3.1, we construct pipeline consisting of prior prediction, sparse rollouts, deviation testing and fusion, and dynamic budget allocation. The underlying logic of this design addresses the statistical tradeoff inherent in the exploration-exploitation dilemma within the continuous decision spaces. Under extreme sparse rollouts scenarios, although the empirical mean vk is unbiased, its signal-to-noise ratio is exceedingly low. If the system relies exclusively on vk to determine whether to allocate additional rollouts, it is highly susceptible to being misled by the variance of single extreme rollout, potentially leading to unbounded computational consumption. The primary objective of introducing the prior in V0.5 is to establish zero-variance statistical anchor without incurring additional rollout budgets. Subsequent mechanisms, including shrinkage fusion and hypothesis testing, essentially quantify the statistical significance of the current empirical distributions deviation from this anchor. When the deviation significantly exceeds the theoretical noise boundary, the system identifies the anchor as invalid, activating the protocol for additional budget allocation. A.2. Bounding Policy Gradient Variance with Baseline MSE (Proof of Theorem 3.1) Objective: In subsection 3.2, we posit that minimizing the MSE of the baseline estimator µ is mathematically equivalent to suppressing the unbounded gradient variance during LLM training. We prove that the trace of the policy gradient covariance matrix is strictly bounded by the baseline MSE. Proof of Theorem 3.1. Let the single-step policy gradient estimator be defined as ˆg(θ) = θ log πθ(ox)(r µ). Because introducing any baseline µ independent of the current action preserves the unbiasedness of the policy gradient (i.e., E[ˆg(θ)] = gtrue), the trace of its covariance matrix is formulated as: Tr(Var(ˆg(θ))) = Eo,µ[ˆg(θ)2] gtrue2 (13) Minimizing the variance is analytically equivalent to minimizing the second moment of the gradient. We introduce the true expected return µtrue into the squared advantage term (r µ)2 and apply algebraic expansion: (r µ)2 = (r µtrue)2 + (µtrue µ)2 + 2(r µtrue)(µtrue µ) (14) Given that the score function θ log πθ(ox) depends exclusively on the policys intrinsic sampling distribution and remains independent of the externally estimated baseline µ, we substitute the expanded equation back into the second moment and decompose it via linearity into three distinct components: 1. Oracle Variance Term (Theoretical Lower Bound): Eo (cid:2)θ log πθ(ox)2(r µtrue)2(cid:3) This term represents the irreducible variance when the baseline is perfectly predicted (µ = µtrue), defined as Varoracle. 2. MSE Propagation Term (Variance Amplifier): Eo,µ (cid:2)θ log πθ(ox)2(µtrue µ)2(cid:3) (15) (16) This component strictly factorizes into the expected squared norm of the score function Φscore = Eo[θ log πθ(ox)2] multiplied by the baseline MSE(µ). For models comprising billions of parameters, Φscore is exceptionally large; consequently, any marginal baseline error undergoes severe proportional amplification. 3. Bias Cross-Perturbation Term (Controlled Penalty): 2Eo,µ (cid:2)θ log πθ(ox)2(r µtrue)(µtrue µ)(cid:3) = 2Eo[. . . ] (Bias(µ)) (17) Defining the absolute value of the trace of the policys intrinsic constant matrix as L/2, the magnitude of this cross term is rigorously bounded by Bias(µ). 14 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Synthesizing the three terms yields the comprehensive upper bound for the policy gradient variance: Tr(Var(ˆg(θ))) Varoracle + Φscore MSE(µ) + Bias(µ) (18) This establishes the theoretical validity of the framework, proving that trading marginally controlled bias for substantial reduction in MSE is mathematically optimal. A.3. Orthogonal Error Decomposition and Optimal Shrinkage Weight (Proof of Theorem 3.2 and Theorem 3.3) Objective: To prove that the MSE of the shrinkage estimator µ = wvk + (1 w)V admits an orthogonal decomposition, and to derive the closed-form optimal weight w. Proof of Theorem 3.2. By definition, the mean squared error is MSE(w) = E[(µ µtrue)2]. Substituting the estimator formulation and partitioning the constant µtrue via 1 = + (1 w) yields: MSE(w) = E[(w(vk µtrue) + (1 w)(V µtrue))2] (19) Expanding the quadratic term: MSE(w) = w2E[(vk µtrue)2] + (1 w)2(V µtrue)2 + 2w(1 w)(V µtrue)E[vk µtrue] (20) Because vk operates as an unbiased estimator (i.e., E[vk µtrue] = 0), the cross term evaluates strictly to 0. Substituting the observational variance σ2 noise and the prior bias 2 results in the orthogonal decomposition: MSE(w) = w2σ2 noise + (1 w)22 (21) Proof of Theorem 3.3. Differentiating the decomposed MSE objective with respect to and setting the derivative to 0: dMSE(w) dw = 2wσ noise 2(1 w)2 = 0 (22) Solving this linear equation yields the unique stationary point = 2 2+σ2 guaranteeing this point is the global minimum. noise . The second derivative is 2σ2 noise + 22 > 0, A.4. Equivalence of Truncation to Hypothesis Testing Objective: To formalize the statistical properties of the empirical bias estimator ˆ2 = max(0, (vk )2 1/k). We demonstrate that this positive-part truncation functionally maps to statistical hypothesis test with defined rejection region. During the fusion of prior knowledge and empirical observations, the primary uncertainty lies in whether the value models prior prediction contains systematic hallucinations (i.e., true 2 > 0). We define the null hypothesis as the absence of systematic error in the prior: H0 : 2 = 0 (i.e., = µtrue) H1 : 2 > 0 Under maximum entropy assumption within binary reward space, the theoretical variance upper bound for single 1/k. Assuming the rollout is exactly 1. Consequently, the variance of the empirical mean vk is tightly bounded by σ2 null hypothesis H0 holds, the expected squared distance between the empirical observation and the prior, (vk )2, must logically equal the pure observation noise: noise E[(vk )2 H0] = E[(vk µtrue)2] = σ noise 1/k (23) The truncation operation max(0, ) executing in real-time precisely mirrors hypothesis testing logic: 1. Acceptance Region: If the divergence satisfies (vk )2 1/k, the system attributes this fluctuation to random noise. = 0). The system discards surface-level error, relying on the Lacking evidence to reject H0, truncation activates ( ˆ2 prior to minimize variance. 2. Rejection Region: If the divergence violates the boundary (vk )2 > 1/k, the realized error exceeds any theoretically plausible statistical noise, enforcing strict rejection of H0. Truncation is bypassed, and the system linearly subtracts the expected noise baseline (vk )2 1/k to isolate the true magnitude of the underlying prior bias. 15 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts A.5. Finite-Sample and Asymptotic Bias Bounds of the Shrinkage Estimator (Proof of Theorem 3.4) Objective: In practical deployment, the adaptive weight ˆwk = acts as random variable correlated with the empirical observation vk. This correlation violates the unbiasedness assumption of static shrinkage. This section quantifies the induced bias Bias(ˆµ) = E[ˆµ] µtrue and proves the two fundamental safety bounds defined in Theorem 3.4. ˆ2 ˆ2 k+1/k Proof of Theorem 3.4. We reconstruct the empirical estimator ˆµ to isolate its unbiased component from the bias-inducing correction term: ˆµ = ˆwk vk + (1 ˆwk)V = vk (1 ˆwk)(vk ) (24) Taking the mathematical expectation of both sides and subtracting the objective expectation µtrue yields an expected error of 0 for the leading term, as vk is strictly unbiased. Thus, all systemic bias is isolated within the correction term: Substituting the analytical formula for the adaptive weight, we define the inner random entity as variable Z: Bias(ˆµ) = [(1 ˆwk)(vk )] = 1/k + 1/k ˆ2 (vk ) (25) (26) The proof objective fundamentally reduces to bounding the absolute value of Z. Property 1 in Theorem 3.4: Next, we leverage the piecewise nature of the positive-part truncation in ˆ2 analyze arbitrary rollout across the entire sample space: 1. Case 1 (No truncation penalty): When (vk )2 1/k, truncation enforces ˆ2 the expression to = vk . Applying the square root to the piecewise condition directly yields: = 0. Substituting this into simplifies to exhaustively 1 (27) 2. Case 2 (Truncation penalty triggered): When (vk )2 > 1/k, truncation is inactive, maintaining ˆ2 = (vk )2 1/k. The denominator of algebraically reduces to exactly (vk )2. Thus: = 1/k (vk )2 (vk ) = 1/k vk (28) Given the active condition vk > 1/ numerator 1/k produces: k, its reciprocal rigorously satisfies 1 vkV < k. Multiplying by the fixed < 1/k 1/ = 1 (29) Synthesizing both cases, the truncation architecture mathematically guarantees that 1/ probability space. Applying the triangle inequality for expectations establishes the absolute bound: almost surely across the Bias(ˆµ) E[Z] 1 (30) Theoretical Guarantee: Regardless of severe observation noise encountered during extreme sparse rollouts, the systemic bias introduced by dynamic fusion is constrained within safe upper envelope, precluding numerical gradient explosion. Property 2 in Theorem 3.4: Assuming an objective prior bias exists ( = µtrue = 0), we introduce minimal error tolerance ϵ = /2 > 0. Leveraging Hoeffdings inequality, we partition the probability space into two mutually exclusive events: 1. Case 1 (typical event, vk µtrue ϵ): The observation converges closely to the objective truth. By the reverse triangle inequality, the surface difference vk is bounded below: vk µtrue vk µtrue 2 = 2 (31) 16 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Given sufficient compute (i.e., 1/ spondingly yields: < /2), the system deterministically stabilizes in Case 2. Scaling correZ = 1/k vk 1/k /2 = 2 (32) Thus, the expected bias contribution from typical events is strictly upper-bounded by O(1/k). 2. Case 2 (Rare Event Ac, vk µtrue > ϵ): According to Hoeffdings inequality, the probability of such severe deviations decays exponentially with rollouts, expressed as P(Ac) = O(eck). Because remains globally bounded by 1/ k, the expected contribution from rare events is: E[Z Ac] P(Ac) 1 O(eck) (33) Because the exponential decay O(eck) approaches zero substantially faster than the polynomial decay 1/k, the total asymptotic limit is entirely dictated by typical events, proving: E[ˆµ] µtrue = (cid:19) (cid:18) 1 (34) k), the targeted systemic Theoretical Guarantee: While standard empirical estimation errors decay at rate of O(1/ bias induced by the framework dissipates at highly aggressive, super-linear rate of O(1/k), establishing mathematically superior variance reduction tradeoff. A.6. Marginal Return Envelope and Optimal Stopping Rule (Proof of Theorem 3.5 and Theorem 3.6) Objective: To formally derive the lower-bound envelope of marginal statistical returns for sequential rollouts (Theorem 3.5) and to establish the continuous optimal stopping boundary for adaptive budget allocation (Theorem 3.6). Proof of Theorem 3.5. The real-time empirical MSE after steps is expressed as: (cid:91)MSE(k) = ˆ2 ˆ2 + 1 (35) Defining the single-step marginal return g(k) as the expected reduction in empirical MSE from one additional rollout, and assuming local smoothness under the One-Step-Look-Ahead (OSLA) framework (i.e., ˆ2 k+1 ˆ2 k), we have: g(k) = (cid:91)MSE(k) (cid:91)MSE(k + 1) = ˆ2 ˆ2 + 1 ˆ2 (k + 1) ˆ2 + 1 = ˆ4 + 1)((k + 1) ˆ2 (k ˆ2 + 1) (36) Since 0 and ˆ2 polynomial with the larger one, generating lower-bound envelope: 0, the denominator factors inherently satisfy (k ˆ + 1) < ((k + 1) ˆ2 + 1). Replacing the smaller g(k) > ˆ4 ((k + 1) ˆ2 + 1)2 (37) This ensures that if this minimal envelope exceeds the cost factor, continuing to rollout remains definitively profitable. Proof of Theorem 3.6. To locate the optimal stopping threshold, we balance the marginal return bound with the normalized marginal compute cost c, and taking the square root yields: And then, we have: ˆ4 ((k + 1) ˆ2 + 1)2 = c, ˆ2 (k + 1) ˆ2 + 1 = (k + 1) ˆ2 + 1 = ˆ2 17 (38) (39) V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts Isolating the continuous boundary variable (k + 1): (k + 1) = 1 1 ˆ2 (40) This closed-form formulation perfectly defines the optimal stopping threshold introduced in Equation 12. Application: This formulation elegantly transitions reinforcement learning compute scheduling into real-time feedback control mechanism. The leading term 1/ operates as the theoretical maximum compute budget constrained by fixed costs. The subtraction term 1/ ˆ2 functions as an adaptive decay penalty driven by real-time observed bias. Severe prior hallucinations diminish the decay term, expanding the boundary toward the maximum budget; conversely, an accurate prior rapidly inflates the decay term, dynamically collapsing the target compute boundary and executing an early halt. A.7. Finite-Cost Regret Bound of Sequential Scheduling Objective: In the dynamic budget allocation process, the system relies on the empirical estimate ˆ2 k, which contains statistical noise, to make real-time decisions on when to halt rollout. Compared to an ideal oracle system that knows the true bias 2 in advance and directly outputs the perfect stopping step oracle, our adaptive scheduling inevitably incurs an excess trial cost. We define this excess cost as the finite-cost regret: Regret(c) = E[R(K )] R(k oracle) (41) where R(k) = (cid:91)MSE(k) + is the total risk function of the system. This part proves that the upper bound of this regret is enveloped by the marginal cost itself, specifically O(c). Proof. Step 1: Apply Taylor expansion to the risk function to quantify the penalty curvature at the optimal stopping point. We extend the discrete system risk function to continuous one: R(x) = 2 x2 + 1 + cx (42) Based on the derivation in Theorem 3.6, the global continuous minimum under the oracle perspective is = 1 2 . We perform second-order Taylor expansion of the risk function at for the actual stopping time . Since the first derivative at the extremum is R(x) = 0, the regret is entirely dominated by the second-order term: 1 Regret(c) 1 2 R(x)E[(K x)2] (43) Analysis: This Taylor expansion strategically decomposes the excess cost into two components: 1. Curvature at the minimum R(x): This represents the steepness of the risk function near the optimal point. steeper curvature implies more severe penalty for deviating from the optimal stopping step. 2. Variance of the stopping step E[(K x)2]: This quantifies how far the actual stopping point deviates from the ideal point due to observation noise. To evaluate the curvature, we take the second derivative of the risk function: R(x) = 26 (x2 + 1)3 (44) Substituting the minimum point x, the denominator simplifies to (2/ heavily dependent on the compute cost c: c)3. Consequently, the curvature at this point is R(x) = 2c3/2 (45) Step 2: Apply the Delta method to translate empirical estimation noise into the MSE of the stopping time. 18 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts The discrepancy between the actual stopping time and the theoretical time originates from replacing the unknown true bias 2 with the noisy empirical bias ˆ2 . To translate this parameter estimation error into decision step error, we apply the Delta method (first-order Taylor expansion) to analyze the fluctuation of the stopping step: (cid:32) 1 1 ˆ2 (cid:33) (cid:19) (cid:18) 1 1 2 = 1 2 1 ˆ ˆ2 2 4 According to statistical estimation theory, near the stopping boundary (where the expected step count is 1/ variance of the empirical variance estimator decays inversely with the rollout size: Var( ˆ2 ) = (cid:19) (cid:18) 1 = O( c) Therefore, the mean squared error of the stopping step induced purely by parameter estimation noise is: E[(K x)2] = O( c) (46) c), the (47) (48) Step 3: Incorporate discrete and nonlinear corrections to establish the final upper bound for the total expected regret. Multiplying the mean squared error O( magnitude of the regret caused by pure parameter estimation error: c) obtained in Step 2 back into the Taylor expansion from Step 1 yields the Regretestimation c3/2 O( c) = O(c2) (49) However, the continuous Taylor expansion above conceals two discrete and nonlinear penalties present in the actual system. The true total regret must superimpose the following correction terms: 1. Discretization Overshoot: The actual rollout step size must be an integer. When the system crosses the continuous theoretical stopping boundary, constant integer overshoot error with mean squared magnitude of O(1) is inevitable. Multiplying this by the curvature c3/2, the regret contributed by this term is O(c3/2). 2. Renewal Correlation: According to nonlinear renewal theory, the decision boundary triggering the stop action is not statistically independent of the current empirical estimator, because the current data dictates the stopping action. This covariance introduces first-order shift. After rigorous mapping, the penalty introduced to the total risk function is strictly bounded at the O(c) scale. Synthesizing these three orthogonal terms, the overall expected regret of the system is: Regret(c) = O(c2) + O(c3/2) + O(c) (50) Analysis: In the context of RL rollouts, the marginal compute cost of single step is inherently minute constant (e.g., = 0.0039). For values strictly less than 1, we have c3/2 c2. Consequently, the lowest-order term O(c) dictates the entire polynomial. Thus, the total system regret is safely enveloped by: Regret(c) O(c) (51) Theoretical and Engineering Significance: This proof establishes robust theoretical guarantee for the deployment of the V0.5 framework. It mathematically demonstrates that abandoning rigid fixed batch size and allowing the system to perform adaptive inference under extreme sparsity and high noise inherently introduces inference errors. However, the expected excess cost incurred to locate the optimal stopping point is merely equivalent to constant number of compute rollouts (strictly proportional to itself, potentially equating to just one or two rollouts). This powerful bound ensures that the system can aggressively pursue high-precision baseline estimation while remaining completely immune to unbounded compute cost explosions caused by dynamic decision errors. 19 V0.5: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts A.8. Analysis of the Base Group Size Objective: To analyze the constraint on the base rollout group size kmin introduced in Theorem 3.6. We derive the lower bound for the minimum initial rollout size to ensure the statistical robustness of the hypothesis testing mechanism within discrete binary reward space. Proof. In the preceding analysis, the truncation mechanism of the empirical bias estimator ˆ2 operates equivalently to hypothesis test in continuous space. Considering the reward space defined in this paper is discrete binary space {1, 1}, if the initial rollout size is overly sparse, the discrete jumps in observations will violate the smooth boundary assumptions of the hypothesis test. Under the binary reward {1, 1}, if there are successful responses within rollouts, the empirical mean is vk = 2x 1. The discrete quantization gap between adjacent possible observation values is: Gap(k) = 2 (52) Simultaneously, the testing threshold, representing the tolerance radius for accepting the prior , is defined by the maximum entropy standard deviation: Threshold(k) = 1 (53) To guarantee the statistical robustness of the hypothesis test, the testing tolerance radius must be greater than or equal to the discrete jump gap induced by single observation change. Failing this, the random fluctuation of single rollout will compel the mean to bypass the entire confidence interval, inciting frequent misjudgments and system instability during high-frequency oscillations. Assuming the value model provides high-confidence prior = 0.8. For small values of k, the system is compromised because the discrete gap strictly exceeds the tolerance radius: When = 1: Gap(1) = 2, and the testing radius is 1. If single rollout yields 1, the empirical error is 1 0.8 = 1.8 > 1, resulting in an immediate rejection. single rollouts noise strictly dominates the test outcome. When = 2: Gap(2) = 1, and the testing radius is approximately 0.707. If rollout yields 1 and 1 outcome, the error is 0 0.8 = 0.8 > 0.707, triggering rejection. Standard variance systematically invalidates the prior. When = 3: Gap(3) 0.667, and the testing radius is approximately 0.577. The discrete gap remains larger than the tolerance radius, perpetuating an unstable testing state. When the base rollout group is increased to = 4, Gap(4) = 0.5, and the testing radius is 1/ 4 = 0.5. The discrete gap identically matches the testing tolerance radius. Continuing with the prior = 0.8: If all 4 rollouts are correct (mean 1): the error is 1 0.8 = 0.2 0.5, accepting . If 3 rollouts are correct and 1 is incorrect (mean 0.5): the error is 0.5 0.8 = 0.3 0.5, which still accepts . This critical threshold establishes foundational statistical buffer zone. single incorrect response generated by the policy model due to inherent variance is appropriately attributed to theoretical random noise, sustaining the validity of the prior and preventing the allocation of redundant compute. For any 4, the system strictly satisfies the necessary condition where the tolerance radius comprehensively covers the discrete gap: 1 2 = 4 (54) Within this regime, the testing tolerance radius is sufficient to absorb at least one complete discrete jump, providing the hypothesis test with rigorous statistical validity. Consequently, within the dynamic budget allocation framework integrating the value model prior and empirical hypothesis testing, the theoretical and engineering minimum initial rollout size is kmin = 4. Allocating an initial budget of less than 4 predictably induces high false rejection rate due to the structural failure of the tolerance radius.
